title: Tamago Gohan (Japanese Egg on Rice)

ingredients.name: short-grain or sona masoori rice, cooked hot
egg, very fresh, at room temperature

subs.name: silken tofu crumbled over rice with soy sauce for a vegan version
tamari for a gluten-free version, or salt with a squeeze of lemon
any neutral oil — sesame oil adds the distinctive aroma

steps.text: Cook the rice in a pot or pressure cooker (2 whistles) with 1.5 cups water until each grain is tender and slightly sticky. The rice must be scorching hot — freshly cooked — for the egg to cook through safely from residual heat.

Serve the hot rice immediately into a bowl. Crack the egg directly over the mound of rice. Beat the egg briefly with chopsticks or a fork right on top of the rice, working fast so the heat of the rice cooks the egg into a creamy, slightly runny layer.
Add the soy sauce, sesame oil and sliced spring onion. Stir and fold the rice through a few more times, mixing the egg coating into every grain. Eat immediately — the dish does not wait.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: soy-sauce → tamari for a gluten-free version, or salt with a squeeze of lemon; gingelly-oil → any neutral oil — sesame oil adds the distinctive aroma (skip if unavailable); egg → silken tofu crumbled over rice with soy sauce for a vegan version
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use ponni, sona masoori or any short-grain rice cooked in a pressure cooker (2 whistles, 1.5 cups water per cup of rice). The key is to use the rice the moment the pressure drops — tamago gohan is ruined by warm, not-hot rice. Soy sauce is widely available at Indian supermarkets; substitute with a pinch of salt and a drizzle of nallennai (gingelly oil) for an entirely local version. Use the freshest eggs you can buy — ideally country eggs (naatu muttai).
